nativeRange.habitat: Clear, fast-flowing, well-oxygenated mountain streams with rocky substrate, abundant aquatic vegetation, and leaf litter; cooler hill and mountain habitats typically above 300 m elevation

decorPreferences: dense planting (java moss, hornwort, elodea, floating plants) for spawning cover
driftwood and leaf litter for natural biotope feel

diet.notes: The White Cloud Mountain Minnow accepts a wide range of standard aquarium foods; quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, and small granules form a suitable staple diet. Regular offerings of frozen or live foods — such as daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, or mosquito larvae — are reported to enhance coloration and condition, and are particularly beneficial when preparing fish for breeding. In the wild the species grazes on algae and biofilm, so spirulina-based foods make a useful supplement. Due to their small mouths, food particles should be kept small; standard flake should be crushed before feeding.

avoidWith: goldfish (predation risk, goldfish outcompete for food)

imageCandidates.notes: Standard FishBase species photo of Tanichthys albonubes showing characteristic red-edged lateral stripe and neon body band.

summary: The White Cloud Mountain Minnow (Tanichthys albonubes) is a widely kept cool-water schooling fish from southern China, recognized by its vivid red-and-gold lateral stripe — earning the nickname 'Poor Man's Neon'. Generally considered among the most beginner-friendly freshwater fish, it thrives without a heater at 16–24°C. Listed as Critically Endangered; rediscovered in Vietnam in 2007.
careNotes: The White Cloud Mountain Minnow is generally considered one of the most beginner-friendly freshwater fish available, and one of very few true cool-water schooling species well suited to unheated indoor aquariums. A school of at least six is recommended to reduce stress and bring out natural schooling behaviour and full colouration. Males are slimmer with more vivid coloration; females are rounder when in spawning condition. The species thrives in the 16–24°C range and tends to become lethargic or susceptible to disease at sustained temperatures above 26°C, making it a poor match for most tropical fish that require heated tanks above that threshold. Strong filtration and good water movement are appreciated, reflecting the species' origin in fast-flowing mountain streams; adequate oxygenation is important. Several selectively bred varieties are available in the trade. The 'Golden' variety lacks the lateral stripe pigmentation and displays a yellow-gold body. The 'Long-fin' (also called Veil-fin) variety carries elongated fins and is reported to be slightly more delicate. The 'Meteor Minnow' is a trade name for a selectively bred long-fin golden form. All three varieties are generally kept under the same care conditions as the standard form. On taxonomy, some authorities place this species in its own family, Tanichthyidae, though placement within Cyprinidae remains the most broadly accepted treatment; both names appear in the scientific literature. The species is listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List. Wild populations in China's Guangdong province remain severely depleted due to habitat loss, and a separate population was rediscovered in Vietnam's Quang Ninh Province in 2007 after being presumed extinct in the wild for decades.
breedingNotes: The White Cloud Mountain Minnow is widely regarded as one of the easiest aquarium fish to breed. Spawning is encouraged by feeding adults frequent small portions of live or frozen foods to bring them into condition. Spawning takes place over fine-leaved plants and moss; eggs are laid individually, and parents tend to show little interest in eating them when plant cover is adequate — a trait that distinguishes this species from many other egg scatterers. A dedicated breeding vessel of 20–30 L, furnished with java moss or a fine-leaved plant and fitted with gentle filtration, is generally recommended; leaving out substrate makes egg collection easier. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 20°C, and the fry are free-swimming within a few days. They initially accept infusoria, vinegar eels, or powdered fry food, then can be transitioned to newly hatched brine shrimp. Conditioned pairs are capable of producing multiple broods per month.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Tanichthys albonubes
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (White Cloud Mountain, Guangdong, China; Vietnam rediscovery 2007), temperature range 14–22°C (operational range widened to 16–24°C per Aquarium Co-Op and FishBase consensus), pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 18–179 ppm (~1–10 dH), adult size to 4 cm, diet (omnivore — algae, small invertebrates), breeding behaviour, and conservation status (Critically Endangered, IUCN 2011 assessment). Taxonomy note: SeriouslyFish places within Cyprinidae; Tanichthyidae treatment also cited in some systematic works but Cyprinidae used here as predominant authority.

additional.name: FishBase — Tanichthys albonubes
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.5 cm TL max), temperature range (16–22°C), native range (China, Vietnam), taxonomy (family Cyprinidae, order Cypriniformes). FishBase lists family as Cyprinidae. Maximum size 4.5 cm TL noted; operational adult range 3–4 cm used as typical aquarium size.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — White Cloud Mountain Minnow Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide. Confirmed temperature range 60–72°F (15–22°C), tank size recommendation (10 gal / ~40 L minimum), schooling group of 6+, omnivore feeding regime, beginner care level. Noted Golden, Long-fin, and Meteor Minnow variety names.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — White Cloud Mountain Minnow
additional.notes: Confirmed cool-water unheated suitability, 'Poor Man's Neon' origin of nickname (brilliant lateral stripe at fraction of neon tetra cost/fragility), critical endangered status and Vietnam 2007 rediscovery (Luu Viet Hung et al.), breeding ease, and variety overview (Golden, Long-fin).

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine — White Cloud Mountain Minnow profile
additional.notes: Historical and aquarium-hobby context: species first described 1932 by Lin Shu-Yen based on specimens collected by Boy Scout Tan (genus named Tanichthys in his honour). Confirmed taxonomy debate between Cyprinidae and Tanichthyidae placements; Cyprinidae used as primary per most recent molecular studies cited in FishBase. Notes on Meteor Minnow as trade variant of long-fin golden selectively bred form.

fish.breedingNotes: Generally spawns readily over java moss or fine-leaved plants; eggs tend to be ignored by adults when plant cover is adequate. Fry accept fine powdered foods and newly hatched brine shrimp. A well-planted dedicated breeding vessel is typically sufficient.
